visible_cue: One Codex session contains a very long AGENTS/instruction block, explicit environment injection, and then real tool-driven work in the same thread.
tricky: A parser that only samples the header sees configuration noise; a parser that only samples later turns loses the long injected context that shaped the session.

visible_cue: The thread still talks about one visible repo path, but the structured cwd field is missing from metadata that usually carries it.
tricky: If linking depends too heavily on structured cwd, this session falls out of the right bucket even though the user still sees a concrete repo in the conversation.

visible_cue: A second host root contains a session that points at the same visible repo path as a local host session.
tricky: If host identity is ignored, two machines collapse into one history; if host identity dominates, the user sees one project split apart for no obvious reason.

visible_cue: Two Codex sessions look like the same product name, `chat-ui-kit`, but one lives under a direct workspace path and the other under docker/codeserver.
tricky: Users often mentally group by visible repo name, while naive path-only linking treats them as different forever and naive basename-only linking may over-merge unrelated copies.

visible_cue: Codex keeps a root history log with repeated continue-style instructions and subagent-related operator prompts outside the transcript session tree.
tricky: If a parser promotes root history to transcript truth, it can create duplicate or low-value turns; if it ignores the file entirely, later automation-loop analysis loses real prevalence evidence.

visible_cue: Claude stores one project directly under a workspace path, and this session contains the same automation-shaped code-review instruction block that appears in another Claude session for the same workspace, plus subagent/tool traffic.
tricky: The visible project path is clean, but the file mixes user asks, repeated meta review prompts, tool traffic, and subagent progress. Retrieval must preserve that this review-style prompt recurs across sessions rather than collapsing it into one generic ask.

visible_cue: A subagent sidechain file sits under the same Claude project tree as the main session.
tricky: Users see it as part of the same working area on disk, but parsers that flatten everything into one thread or ignore sidechains entirely both lose information.

visible_cue: The thread begins with a meta caveat explaining that some messages were generated by local commands and should not be treated like normal conversation, and it repeats the same automation-shaped code-review instruction block seen in another Claude session under the same workspace.
tricky: A parser that only sees role=`user` will over-count meta noise as intent, while a deduplicating retriever may collapse this repeated review-style prompt with the other Claude session and lose explainability about which session produced which ask.

visible_cue: Claude keeps a root history log with slash commands and short follow-up prompts that point at the same visible workspace as the main session tree.
tricky: The file is real evidence about operator flow, but it is not automatically the canonical transcript source. Treating it like a full conversation can duplicate or distort user-turn recall.

visible_cue: A Factory Droid session stores its transcript as JSONL next to a `.settings.json` sidecar that carries model and cumulative token metadata for the same visible workspace.
tricky: If a parser reads only the conversation file it misses sidecar evidence; if it trusts only settings it loses turn boundaries, tool calls, and hidden thinking fragments.

visible_cue: An AMP thread keeps the visible workspace only in the root env tree metadata while the actual tool exchange is spread across later message objects in one whole-thread JSON file.
tricky: If a parser only samples message text it loses the workspace root; if it only reads the root metadata it misses the user ask, tool roundtrip, and unsupported content that should stay auditable.

visible_cue: AMP also keeps a root history log with short repeated follow-up prompts outside the main thread JSON surface.
tricky: If the product later needs automation prevalence review, this history file matters; if it is treated as transcript-primary, it can over-count low-information prompts such as continue or numeric acknowledgements.

visible_cue: Cursor has one workspaceStorage directory whose visible project path is the same path another antigravity directory points to.
tricky: If app identity is ignored, cross-app state for one visible path collapses together; if app identity is absolute, users cannot see one project’s history across tools.

visible_cue: Two opaque Cursor storage IDs look like project session directories, but neither has workspace.json and both only point back to Cursor settings state.
tricky: A parser that treats every storage ID as a project invents false projects; a parser that drops them entirely hides uncertainty that a user may want surfaced.

visible_cue: Cursor also keeps per-agent chat stores under `.cursor/chats/<workspace-hash>/<agent-id>/store.db`, where one hex-encoded `meta.value` points at opaque blob rows containing prompt, reasoning, and assistant payload fragments.
tricky: A parser that treats this as the already-supported Cursor editor-state path will misclassify the source; a parser that samples only ASCII strings can recover text hints while still missing the structure needed for faithful session reconstruction.

visible_cue: One Cursor workspace-hash directory can contain multiple `agent-id/store.db` files, each with its own `meta` row and blob graph.
tricky: If the workspace hash alone is treated as one session, multiple chat threads collapse together; if each opaque blob is treated as a standalone turn, the real per-agent grouping disappears.

visible_cue: Antigravity points at the same visible path as the Cursor sample, but keeps different app artifacts and state.
tricky: This is the user-facing version of ‘same folder, different app state’: identical visible root, different app-specific storage shape.

visible_cue: One antigravity storage directory mixes current DB, backup DB, workspace.json, terminal state, and an unrelated Python startup sidecar in the same folder.
tricky: Users see all of these files side-by-side. Parsers that hardcode one ‘main’ file miss useful evidence; parsers that ingest everything equally overfit editor/plugin noise.

visible_cue: Two antigravity storage directories both look like `AI roadmap`, but they live under different parent paths and carry different recent file histories.
tricky: Users often identify projects by the last path segment. Grouping by basename merges unrelated roots; grouping by full path can hide that both folders look like the same project name.

visible_cue: Two different antigravity storage IDs both point at the exact same visible `chat-ui-kit` path, but one directory has backup and terminal state while the other is much thinner.
tricky: Treating storage ID as identity splits one visible project into multiple islands; blindly merging by visible path can hide that one directory contains richer or older evidence than the other.

visible_cue: One antigravity directory points at a single visible repo, but its current DB and backup DB disagree on editor history and restored state.
tricky: A parser that only trusts `state.vscdb` or only trusts `.backup` loses real evidence about crash recovery, restore flows, and what the user recently had open.

visible_cue: Antigravity also keeps per-cascade brain artifacts under `~/.gemini/antigravity/brain`, including task files, plans, walkthroughs, browser scratchpads, and conversation-history snapshots referenced by workspace state.
tricky: If a parser only scans workspaceStorage it misses these artifact files; if it treats every artifact as a user turn it over-weights assistant-authored plans and walkthroughs.

visible_cue: With the Antigravity desktop app running, the local language server exposes trajectory summaries and step streams whose `userInput.userResponse` contains the raw prompt text, including short follow-ups like `Continue`.
tricky: A parser that trusts titles, `items[].text`, or brain markdown over the live `userResponse` field will rewrite the user's text or promote review artifacts into turns.

visible_cue: Gemini CLI keeps one project label at `~/.gemini/projects.json` while both `history/<project>/` and `tmp/<project>/` carry `.project_root` sidecars that point back to the visible workspace.
tricky: A parser that only reads session JSON misses workspace/project evidence; a parser that scans all of `.gemini` without filtering may confuse Gemini CLI state with unrelated Antigravity artifacts.

visible_cue: One Gemini CLI project stores transcript JSON under `~/.gemini/tmp/<project>/chats/`, with the user-authored prompt preserved inside `messages[].content[].text`.
tricky: A parser that only sees `.project_root` sidecars finds the workspace but no turns; a parser that only sees the chat file loses project-mapping context and may not distinguish Gemini CLI files from other `.gemini` families.

visible_cue: A real-archive Gemini tmp project can contain `chats/*.json` plus `logs.json` while carrying no `.project_root` sidecars and no matching `projects.json` entry.
tricky: A parser that assumes companion metadata is mandatory will drop valid sessions; a parser that ignores `logs.json` loses operator-visible command history that sits alongside the chats.

visible_cue: One hashed Gemini tmp project can accumulate multiple chat JSON sessions plus a shared `logs.json` activity stream even when no workspace-mapping companions exist.
tricky: A parser that samples only one chat file misses scale behavior; a parser that treats the hash directory as noise loses the only stable local grouping visible in the archive.

visible_cue: A CodeBuddy project JSONL can mix `providerData.skipRun` local-command echoes with the actual CLI transcript lines that carry the real user request and assistant reply.
tricky: A parser that promotes every user row equally will over-count command noise; a parser that ignores `providerData` loses the distinction between local shell echoes and the real chat turn.

visible_cue: One CodeBuddy project directory can contain both zero-byte JSONL files and non-empty transcript JSONL files, plus local storage companions that reflect recent commands.
tricky: A parser that treats every JSONL filename as a real session invents empty conversations; a parser that ignores sibling files misses that empty and non-empty captures coexist under one visible project root.

visible_cue: One OpenClaw agent stores a typed event-stream session under `agents/main/sessions/*.jsonl`, with explicit session cwd, model changes, assistant thinking, tool calls, and tool results in the same transcript.
tricky: A parser that expects flat `role` + `content` JSONL rows will miss the real event taxonomy, nested `message` payloads, `toolResult` role, and `session.cwd` workspace signal that actually anchor the session.

visible_cue: The same OpenClaw `sessions/` tree can contain active transcripts plus `.reset.*` and `.deleted.*` lifecycle variants, and one deleted session records an `openclaw:prompt-error` custom event.
tricky: A parser that treats lifecycle-suffixed files as generic noise loses evidence about resets, deletions, and prompt-error behavior; a parser that blindly promotes them as ordinary active sessions may double-count history without a lifecycle rule.

visible_cue: OpenClaw writes standalone cron-run JSONL outside the main session transcript tree, but the records still point back to the related session and summarize the automated run.
tricky: A discovery pass that only reads `agents/*/sessions/*.jsonl` misses this evidence completely, while a parser that treats cron-run summaries like normal transcript turns invents human asks that never happened.

visible_cue: Inside `~/.openclaw/agents/`, some agent directories may expose only companion config like `auth-profiles.json` while another agent carries the real session transcripts.
tricky: A discovery pass that treats every agent root as transcript-bearing will invent empty session families; a parser that ignores agent config entirely can miss model/profile evidence that explains provider labels without being the canonical transcript stream.

visible_cue: The session metadata lives under storage/session/global, but the actual prompt, plan text, and tool trace live in per-message part files.
tricky: A parser that only reads session JSON and message headers loses the user prompt and the assistant tool context because the real content sits under storage/part/<message-id>/*.json.

visible_cue: A second OpenCode session shows up with session metadata plus a todo sidecar even though no transcript messages are present beside it.
tricky: If every adjacent JSON file is treated as transcript content, todo planning state pollutes the conversation projection; if companions are ignored entirely, real operator-facing plan state disappears from evidence review.

visible_cue: One Claude transcript session repeats `continue` three times in the same session before the user finally asks a substantive follow-up.
tricky: A loop-aware projection should not mistake every short follow-up pair for a flood, but it also should not let three near-identical low-information prompts drown out the later real ask.

visible_cue: Claude root history now shows the same workspace/session issuing `continue` repeatedly outside the transcript tree.
tricky: This proves prevalence for loop-like operator behavior, but these rows must remain secondary evidence rather than becoming canonical grouped turns.

visible_cue: Codex root history now includes three `continue` rows for the same session id, not just one isolated prompt.
tricky: The pattern is useful for prevalence review and fixture coverage, but it should not leak into transcript-primary grouping because the file remains secondary evidence.

visible_cue: AMP root history now repeats `continue` for the same visible cwd, showing loop-like operator behavior outside the main thread JSON file.
tricky: This keeps AMP in the family-wide loop review without pretending that root history is transcript truth.

visible_cue: One OpenClaw cron-run file now shows repeated summaries for the same hourly job and linked main session.
tricky: The file proves automation flood prevalence and lineage shape, but those repeated summaries must remain secondary evidence rather than synthetic user turns.
